data.title: Protalix: Elfabrio Struggles Against Oral Competition
data.description:
Protalix BioTherapeutics, Inc. (PLX) wants to become a notable player in the Gaucher disease and Fabry disease therapeutics markets.

But on March 18, it released disappointing figures for Q4.

Let me remind you about it.

Total revenue of Protalix fell 49.9% from $17.85 million in Q3 last year to $9.12 million, missing the consensus estimate by $5.24 million.

And its non-GAAP EPS also came in below the consensus estimate, as in the past 4 quarters, but this time by 8 cents.

Thus, is the competition in this therapeutic area and other risks, which I'll discuss later, justifying a Hold or Buy rating, according to my ALLKA 4D rating?

The partnership with Chiesi leaves much to be desired

On October 18, 2017, Protalix signed a deal worth up to $370 million with Chiesi to develop PRX-102, now known as Elfabrio [pegunigalsidase alfa], and commercialize it outside the U.S.

Then, on July 24 of the next year, Protalix expanded the agreement, granting the rights of Elfabrio in the U.S., under which it is eligible to receive up to $805 million.

And speaking in simple words, pegunigalsidase alfa is an ERT [enzyme replacement therapy] approved two years ago, on May 10, by the FDA for the treatment of Fabry disease. For this milestone, Protalix received $20 million.

And by the way, 5 days earlier, on May 15, the European Commission also gave the green light to Elfabrio for this indication.

But before I analyze the performance of this ERT, I want to briefly explain what Fabry disease is.

It is a rare inherited disorder caused by deficient activity of an enzyme called alpha-GAL [alpha-galactosidase A].

When this enzyme is deficient, the body is unable to break down the fatty substance, globotriaosylceramide.

Then, this substance accumulates in cells, leading to life-threatening problems, including an enlarged heart, irregular heartbeat, myocardial infarction, and kidney failure.

Well, according to Orphanet, the global prevalence of Fabry disease at birth is about 1 case per 15,000, which, in my view, is one of the factors that attracts Big Pharma to develop medications against it.

But that's not all.

Also, most approved drugs have an "orphan drug designation," which gives them a seven-year exclusivity period after approval.

To my regret, Elfabrio has a problem here.

I could not find any mention of it in the FDA's databases.

And more importantly, the multidisciplinary review explicitly states that PLX/Chiesi's drug has no orphan designation.

On the other hand, using information from Protalix's 10-K and Google Patents, I found that of the current patents covering Elfabrio, only US9194011B2 [or PCT/IL2011/000209] is active.

It expires on November 17, 2030.

Yes, Protalix filed application number PCT/Il2018/050018 in 2018, but it has not yet been approved as you can see below (highlighted in orange).

Thus, I believe pegunigalsidase alfa's patent protection is far from perfect. However, this could change if PCT/Il2018/050018 is approved, which will protect Elfabrio from “cheap copies” until 2038.

And given everything I've said so far, did Chiesi make a mistake by making a deal with Protalix BioTherapeutics?

The only thing that can help answer this question is pegunigalsidase alfa sales.

So, it generated about $3.96 million for Protalix in Q4, down 74.1% year-on-year and 54.8% QoQ. Moreover, as you can see in the chart below, Elfabrio sales began to decline sharply in the second half of 2024.

I believe the key reason for this negative trend is competition with BioMarin/Amicus's Galafold [migalastat] (BMRN) (FOLD), which has a strong competitive advantage: it's administered orally, rather than being injected every 2 weeks, as in the case with Elfabrio.

This is reflected in Galafold sales, which totaled $150.2 million in Q4, up 17.8% year-on-year.

Sales of Elelyso are under pressure in the Gaucher disease treatment market

I believe the revenue miss, quite substantial, is also due to weaker demand for Elelyso [taliglucerase alfa] outside of Brazil.

This enzyme replacement therapy [ERT] is commercialized by Pfizer (PFE) under a deal concluded more than 15 years ago, on November 30, 2009.

Protalix's revenue from Elelyso sales was about $2.8 million, down 0.3% QoQ. In my view, this drop reflects increased competition in the Gaucher disease drugs market, mainly from Sanofi's Cerezyme/Cerdelga (SNY).

In my opinion, its performance can be called catastrophic, especially considering that Elelyso was first approved by the FDA on May 1, 2012.

In general, I'd also like to point out before moving on to the "Risks" section that Elelyso is also being commercialized in Brazil in partnership with Fiocruz. But things are much worse there.

Taliglucerase alfa's net revenue was only $1.94 million.

Risks

Now, it's time for my table with key risks.

One of them is the decline in Protalix's total cash and short-term investments in the fourth quarter by $4.5 million year-on-year to $30.3 million.

Well, it received $25 million from Chiesi following the EC's approval of a every-4-weeks dosing regimen for Elfabrio on March 9.

But given PLX's historical rate of cash burn, as well as increased R&D costs due to the development of PRX-115 and PRX-119, it may require a cash infusion, for example, through a secondary public offering in Q4 2027.

Takeaway

But, in addition to Elelyso and Elfabrio, Protalix has two product candidates, PRX-115 and PRX-119.

Here, I want to focus your attention on PRX-115, since PRX-119 is still in preclinical studies.

PRX-115 is a chemically modified enzyme, uricase, being developed to treat a painful form of arthritis called uncontrolled gout.

And one of the causes of gout is when levels of uric acid in the blood rise above 6 mg/dL.

All this ultimately leads to the deposition of monosodium urate crystals between joints.

Yes, I agree that in a Phase 1 study [NCT05745727], PRX-115 demonstrated that it reduced urate concentrations below 6.0 mg/dL.

But again, the problem is in the details. Only two of its most concentrated doses [Cohort 6 and Cohort 7] showed strong results.

What I mean is that a single dose of PRX-115 at these doses maintained levels at 6 mg/dL for up to six weeks after administration.

And this is important.

Because firstly, it must have at least some competitive advantage over Amgen's Krystexxa (AMGN), which is administered once every 2 weeks. By the way, its sales were $435 million in Q4, up 35.9% quarter-over-quarter.

However, given that Protalix's cash and short-term investments at the end of December were only $30.3 million, with a negative EBIT, this is a problem for investors.

Results of the Phase 2 trial [NCT07280156] for PRX-115 won't be available until at least 2028.

And without strong clinical data and the weak sales of Elelyso/Elfabrio, I believe few institutional investors will want to invest in Protalix now.

As a result, I believe Protalix stock's risk/reward profile is unfavorable, especially given its stock price has risen 41.7% over the past 6 months.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Richemont: 3 Factors To Consider Before Buying
data.description:
2026 hasn't gone well for Compagnie Financière Richemont SA's (OTCPK:CFRUY) (OTCPK:CFRHF) so far. It's seen a bigger decline than both the S&P Global Luxury Index (SPGLGUN) and the S&P 500 (SP500) (see Chart 1 below). Disappointing as this is, two trends provide some comfort:

  • CFRUY's drop is better placed than the other three big luxury fashion stocks, Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY), LVMH (OTCPK:LVMUY), and Kering (OTCPK:PPRUY), in that it has seen a smaller drop (see Chart 2 below).
  • The weakness in price performance for the luxury set started in earnest only from February end, when the US-Iran war began, indicating that it's more event- than fundamentals-driven.

These raise the question of whether CFRUY can see a turnaround now. This question acquires validity in light of the notable 12% gains for the stock in the past month, even as it still trades below levels seen at the start of 2026. The answer is both yes and no, as there are arguments on both sides. These arguments are based on three key factors that can determine what's next for the stock are discussed below.

#1. Luxury Industry in the Macro Context

As peace still remains elusive, increased macroeconomic uncertainty that has already shown up in an inflation uptick calls for a relook at the best stocks to buy. Luxury stocks' relatively slowdown-proof nature certainly makes them attractive, but there's another side to the story too. As discussed below.

The Upside: Unlike other consumer discretionary stocks, luxury stocks are ones to watch, if not outright buy, in times of macroeconomic uncertainty. Like right now. This uncertainty's first sign was the oil price shock, which then resulted in a jump in inflation numbers in March. From a big picture perspective, even though inflation is still in check, until peace returns for good, there's a risk to it.

Luxury companies like Richemont are well placed to manage this risk, with their healthy operating profit margin at 20% for FY25 (year ending March 2025). Not only does it have the leeway to absorb higher costs, but it can, importantly, pass on costs more easily to consumers as well.

The Downside: The argument might hold true during regular times, but what of when there's a luxury market slowdown underway already? Since the post-COVID-19 spending spree ended, the luxury sector has been firmly in slowdown mode over the past couple of years, with signs of recovery visible only in recent months.

One of the arguments for the luxury market slowdown is the increase in prices when demand was high. According to a McKinsey and Company report, 80% of the growth in the industry over the 2019 to 2023 period was on account of price increases, while volume growth contributed far less. However, since then, price increases started backfiring. As the same report notes, higher prices led to reduced demand. The key point here is that even with strong margins, luxury companies might not have pricing power this time around if inflation rises.

#2. Sales Growth Recovery

Richemont's highlight feature is its resilient sales, which have continued to grow despite market weakness. But geopolitical stresses combined with macroeconomic disturbances do put revenues at risk, as discussed below.

The Upside: At a time when the luxury market is in a slump, as evident from LVMH's nearly unchanged organic revenues in 2025 and Kering's even worse performance last year, Richemont's continued growth is encouraging. Sure, it has slowed down. In FY23, for example, sales grew by a robust 19%, a number that dwindled to just 4% by FY25. However, even the fact that it continues to remain well in the positive territory is a positive indeed.

Moreover, signs of a robust recovery underway are obvious. In Q3 FY26 (October-December 2025), Richemont's sales growth at constant prices was at 11% YoY. This was a slight improvement from the 10% YoY increase seen in H1 FY26. Specifically, continued growth in its big Asia Pacific market was encouraging since it reflects well on the important Chinese market.

The Downside: There are risks to growth, though, most significantly from the Middle East & Africa market, which brings in some 10% of the company's revenues. In Q3 FY26, it saw the highest growth among all regions of 20% YoY, continuing the trend from H1 FY26. However, with the US-Iran war underway, which has affected the Middle East significantly, weakness in the region is possible for Q4 FY26. Also, less concerning but still worth pointing out is some softening in European growth to 8% YoY (H1 FY26: 11% YoY).

#3. Two Sides to the Market Multiples

To estimate the forward P/E for FY26, here I've left the assumptions unchanged from last time. The projection for profit for continuing operations, which is also assumed to double as the profit for the period this past financial year, is at EUR 3.9 billion (USD 4.6 billion), and the forward P/E ratio is now at 25.1x.

For FY27, revenue growth is assumed to remain at 5% at actual exchange rates in FY27, the same as that for FY26. However, the profit margin is reduced from 18% earlier to 17.2% now. This is because the earlier underlying assumption that costs will continue to soften on falling inflation no longer stands. The profit figure for FY27 comes to EUR 4.1 billion (USD 4.8 billion), and the forward P/E to 23.9x.

The upside: CFRUY's forward P/E doesn't look bad at all when compared to peers. The average forward ratio for the big four luxury fashion stocks (including CFRUY) is at 31.7x for the present year, indicating that CFRUY is undervalued. Further, it remains below the luxury average of 25.8x for the next year as well, though to a smaller extent.

The Downside: Compared with its own past average, however, the stock does less well. CFRUY's five-year average sits at 21x, which indicates that for both FY26 and FY27, the stock is overpriced.

What's Next?

With its neat gains in the past month coupled with continued weakness YTD, it can be tempting to buy CFRUY now. But as the discussion above reveals, there are arguments both for and against buying the stock.

The company's sales growth recovery, its market multiples compared with peers, and even the potential for luxury market resilience in a slowdown work well for it. But the ongoing luxury market slump, potential weakness in its fast-growing Middle East and Africa market, as well as high P/E compared with its own past averages, temper the case for it.

I'm retaining a Hold rating on the stock until its FY26 results release in May, which can provide a fuller picture of at least what to expect in the current financial year. Also, by then hopefully the geopolitical stresses would have resolved to provide greater market stability. But we have to wait and watch.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen | Michael Feldman, CFA | Michael Testorf, CFA | Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Resetting as Growth Becomes More Attractive

Market and Performance Overview

International equities declined meaningfully in March as the U.S. and Israel’s military conflict with Iran escalated materially through the month, leading to mixed results for the first quarter after two positive months. The core benchmark MSCI EAFE Index finished down 1.2% yet extended its leadership over the S&P 500 Index (-4.3%). Value stocks outside the U.S. continued to exhibit strength, with the MSCI EAFE Value Index managing a gain of 2.0% for the quarter, outperforming the MSCI EAFE Growth Index (-4.7%) by 670 basis points. Value is ahead of growth by over 1,700 bps for the trailing 12 months.

While international markets had been preparing for a period of lower rates, which would have been beneficial to growth stocks generally, rates began to move higher following the start of the Middle East conflict. Equity markets are still grappling with long-term implications of these moves and, most importantly, their duration.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

With continued headwinds from value leadership, especially in energy, the ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy underperformed its primary MSCI EAFE benchmark but outperformed its secondary MSCI EAFE Growth benchmark.

The biggest obstacle affecting relative performance was a lack of traditional energy exposure in a three-month period which saw the energy sector soar 40%. Given the selloff in March, higher-beta names in areas such as financials, consumer discretionary and information technology (IT) names were most impacted.

"We are seeking out companies supporting energy security and benefiting from increased fiscal spending in Europe."

The Iran conflict created immediate oil and gas supply disruptions that directly affect an energy-dependent Europe already struggling with the cutoff of Russian energy due to the war in Ukraine. Closure of the Strait of Hormuz also raised the risk of higher inflation from commodity price spikes and caused the European Central Bank to reverse course on what was expected to be a continued program of rate cuts. These rapid and dramatic changes, combined with profit taking after strong performance in 2025, sent the financials sector down 3.7% for the quarter and pressured portfolio holdings Adyen (ADYEY), Intesa Sanpaolo (ISNPY) and NatWest Group (NWG).

Banks, particularly European banks — which represent our largest exposure — saw some profit taking after a standout 2025 and have also served as a source of funding for banks in other regions, insurance companies and stock exchanges, all of which are perceived as more defensive. Notably, Australian and Canadian banks have shown resilience, being less affected by rising oil and gas prices or potential rationing. Over the quarter, we trimmed some of our banking exposure as several holdings approached their target prices.

The launch of Claude Cowork and other generative AI tools in the quarter heightened concerns that AI-native solutions could alter competitive dynamics in IT, increase pricing pressure, extend the sales cycle or compress long-term growth rates. As a result, the software sector — historically viewed as stable and durable due to high recurring revenue and strong retention — experienced broad-based multiple contraction. This dynamic impacted nearly all software companies, including those we believe are relatively well-positioned to benefit from AI or are less susceptible to disruption. We broadly reduced the portfolio’s software exposure in the quarter, including cutting Germany’s SAP (SAP) by roughly two-thirds.

Despite this short-term weakness, we are optimistic about the outlook for international growth stocks and confident that the work we have put into the portfolio in recent quarters positions the Strategy very well for improved outcomes going forward. After an extended period of value outperformance, we believe growth stocks have given up much of their valuation premium.

Exhibit 2: International Growth Premiums Have Compressed

Portfolio Positioning

We saw indiscriminate selling in March across our investable universe, especially among what we consider emerging growth companies with higher revenue growth and innovative business models that carry higher risk. We used the spike in volatility to repurchase shares of video streamer Spotify (SPOT). Prior to the conflict-driven selloff, we exited Shopify (SHOP) and MercadoLibre (MELI) as they reached our price targets.

We are also seeking out companies supporting energy security and benefiting from increased fiscal spending in Europe; these include defense stocks and those enabling electric grid infrastructure such as new buy French utility Engie (ENGIY). Supporting our expectation of higher infrastructure spending in Europe as well was the purchase of U.K.-based global mining company Rio Tinto (RIO), the world’s largest miner by revenue and second largest by profit. While its business has historically been dominated by iron ore, more than half of its future profits will be generated from copper, aluminum, lithium and other industrial metals in high demand.

In addition, we continued to move more meaningfully into Japan with five new positions that reflect our confidence in a number of tailwinds: the country is an outlier in developed markets in that interest rates are rising, which should meaningfully boost net interest margins for banks; ongoing governance reforms are driving higher returns on equity and increasing shareholder-friendly actions such as stock buybacks; and expansionary fiscal policy under the consolidating Takaichi administration that should support domestic demand, defense spending and technology investment.

We purchased ORIX (IX), which provides capital and financing to public and private sector clients and operates its own real estate and renewable energy assets; Pan Pacific International Holdings (DQJCY), an operator of discount retail chains; Shin-Etsu Chemical (SHECY), a supplier of specialty materials to the semiconductor, construction and pharmaceutical sectors; and Sumitomo Electric Industries (SMTOY), a manufacturer of electrical components that power grid connection, transmission and data networking equipment. A portion of these buys came were funded from the sale of European aerospace and defense contractor Airbus (EADSF) which was approaching our price target.

Within IT, we are roughly neutral to the semiconductor capital equipment industry after taking some profits in the strong-performing ASML (ASML). We diversified exposure to this critical picks and shovels area of AI buildouts with the first-quarter additions of ASM International (ASMIY) and Lasertec (LSRCY). ASM is the leader in atomic layer deposition, a precise deposition technique required in the most advanced semiconductors. The Dutch company’s main clients are logic foundries Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Samsung (SSNLF) and Intel (INTC), as well as memory foundries SK Hynix and Micron Technology (MU). Foundries and wafer fab equipment providers like the ASM work closely, which gives the firm insight into the customer's innovation and product road maps. Japan’s Lasertec is a pure-play leader in inspection and measurement equipment that is well-positioned to benefit from the emerging growth drivers of extreme ultraviolet (EUV) memory adoption and AI infrastructure. We took advantage of an attractive entry point given that the company is at trough earnings with order recovery ahead.

The sales of Japanese IT consultant Nomura Research Institute (NRILY) and U.K. business information provider RELX (RELX) were driven in part by negative sentiment over AI disintermediation risks that are currently overwhelming the fundamentals of these businesses. We exited Israeli security software maker Check Point (CHKP) because our thesis on revenue growth acceleration is taking longer to play out than expected.

Outlook

War has changed cost economics around the world, which we know is not good for Europe due to its energy dependence, as we saw during the early stages of the Russia-Ukraine war. How long this lasts and what the result will be has yet to be determined, especially with Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz, and we must look at potential impacts across several areas. We are analyzing how inflationary pressures from higher commodity prices could impact consumers, as well as what they mean for input prices for industrial and materials companies.

We have also been carefully vetting the portfolio to be better in tune with the current risk environment and ensure we are appropriately positioned for the next leg of growth. We have reduced our IT overweight while continuing to add to utilities and materials. We see these structural companies as the new growth stocks and have seen early signs validating our thesis. We have also been moving more meaningfully into Japan, and to a lesser extent Australia, highlighting an overall priority to add in places where we see inexpensive valuations and rising earnings.

While the Strategy has very limited exposure to oil and gas stocks — which acted as a major performance headwind in the quarter — we are also looking at our energy exposure, achieved through owning utilities and select industrials, as a way to play defense and be well-positioned to participate in a period of surging power demand. This contributed to our decision to add a position in Australian LNG company Woodside Energy during the quarter.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge International Growth ADR Strategy underperformed its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark but outperformed its MSCI EAFE Growth Index secondary benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy produced positive contributions across three of the 11 sectors in which it was invested: industrials, materials and utilities. The consumer discretionary and financials sectors were the chief detractors.

We have been monitoring on a regular basis and reporting to our clients on the performance of the MSCI EAFE (core), MSCI EAFE Value and MSCI EAFE Growth indexes in order to provide context on market movements for growth investors.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Index, overall stock selection and sector allocation detracted from performance. In particular, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials, communication services and IT, an underweight to energy and an overweight to consumer discretionary weighed on results. On the positive side, stock selection in the industrials and health care sectors proved beneficial.

Relative to the MSCI EAFE Growth Index, overall sector allocation contributed to performance. In particular, overweights to materials and utilities, an underweight to IT and stock selection in industrials and health care supported results. Conversely, stock selection in consumer discretionary, financials, consumer staples and IT and an underweight to industrials hurt performance.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to returns relative to the MSCI EAFE Index in the quarter were ASML in IT, Siemens Energy and BAE Systems in industrials, Agnico Eagle Mines in materials, and AstraZeneca in health care. The greatest relative detractors were Sea Limited in consumer discretionary, Nomura Research and SAP in IT, Tencent in communication services as well as Adyen in financials.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we repurchased shares in Deutsche Boerse in financials. The Strategy also closed positions in Argenx in health care, Celestica in IT and Ferrari in consumer discretionary.

Elisa Mazen, Managing Director, Head of Global Growth, Portfolio Manager

Michael Feldman, CFA, Director, Portfolio Manager

Michael Testorf, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Pawel Wroblewski, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Copyright © 2026 ClearBridge Investments. All opinions and data included in this commentary are as of the publication date and are subject to change. The opinions and views expressed herein are of the author and may differ from other portfolio managers or the firm as a whole, and are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. This information should not be used as the sole basis to make any investment decision. The statistics have been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the accuracy and completeness of this information cannot be guaranteed. Neither ClearBridge Investments, LLC nor its information providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Performance source: Internal. Benchmark source: Morgan Stanley Capital International. Neither ClearBridge Investments, LLC nor its information providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Performance is preliminary and subject to change. Neither MSCI nor any other party involved in or related to compiling, computing or creating the MSCI data makes any express or implied warranties or representations with respect to such data (or the results to be obtained by the use thereof), and all such parties hereby expressly disclaim all warranties of originality, accuracy, completeness, merchantability or fitness for a particular purpose with respect to any of such data. Without limiting any of the foregoing, in no event shall MSCI, any of its affiliates or any third party involved in or related to compiling, computing or creating the data have any liability for any direct, indirect, special, punitive, consequential or any other damages (including lost profits) even if notified of the possibility of such damages. No further distribution or dissemination of the MSCI data is permitted without MSCI’s express written consent. Further distribution is prohibited.

Performance source: Internal. Benchmark source: Standard & Poor's.
